summary: World Cup debutants (FIFA 63rd) under Jamal Sellami — unlikely to dominate the ball (41% average possession in qualifying) but a real punch on the counter from a deep block. A 3-4-3 that drops into a back five, giving a strong defensive foundation.
style: 3-4-3 that becomes a 5-4-1 / back five out of possession; direct to a rotating attacking trident — Al-Taamari and Olwan operating behind Al-Naimat — with quick combinations and runs in behind on the break (Olwan's goal vs Oman came barely 10 seconds after a throw-in).

counterReliance.note: Deep back-five block, counter at speed through the front three

strengths: Counter-attacks at speed from a compact, organised deep block
A potent, rotating front three — 24 goals between Al-Taamari, Olwan and Al-Naimat in 16 Asian qualifiers
Sharp close combinations to unlock defences quickly in transition
weaknesses: Big defensive gaps if the back five sits too deep
Limited possession and creativity against teams that sit in against them

keyPlayers.name: Musa Al-Taamari
keyPlayers.note: The talisman — a left-footer who shoots from range (a third of his efforts from outside the box), with a free kick vs Nigeria and a goal-of-the-season volley vs Lyon to his name

keyPlayers.name: Ali Olwan
keyPlayers.note: Designated penalty taker on 27 international goals — three more would match the all-time record, four would break it; a chance to make history

tactics: Defend deep in a back five, stay compact, and break fast through the front three — quick combinations and runs in behind. The gaps when the block drops too low are the vulnerability, but they pack a counter-attacking punch on their World Cup debut.
notes: Every Jordan player's first World Cup — first goal, assist or booking enters the history books.
sources: The Athletic — 2026 World Cup tactical guide, Group J (5 Jun 2026)
John Walker (@johnwalker_1986) — pre-tournament tactical scouting, May–Jun 2026
